I appreciate the perspective, and it is entirely understandable why the recent margin compression and cash flow volatility might look concerning at first glance. But based on the Q3 and Q4 numbers, the idea that $MELI growth is uneconomic misses the deeper strategy. The revenue growth is very real, not artificial.

MercadoLibre reached $8.76 billion in net revenues in Q4 2025, driven by a massive 751.8 million actual items sold. It is true that they are actively subsidizing the cost of these transactions, such as lowering the free shipping threshold in Brazil. But this is not a sign of a struggling business. Instead, it is a deliberate choice to trade short-term operating margins for long-term market share. They are using their massive, capital-intensive logistics network to push out competitors.

Looking only at gross profit is also the wrong way to measure their true economic engine. The heaviest costs, like marketing and credit expansion, sit in operating expenses. To really understand the business, you have to look at their NIMAL and operating income. Their credit portfolio just hit $12.5 billion in Q4. This is not cash burn. It is the acquisition of highly profitable financial assets. The early cohorts of these credit cards are already maturing into profitability.

Focusing only on the fierce competition in Brazil completely ignores their massive structural advantages elsewhere. It misses that over 20% of MercadoLibre's total revenue comes from Argentina, where they operate a near-absolute monopoly. On top of that, they enjoy a very healthy duopoly in Mexico. Mexico is their biggest growth story right now, and their integrated Mercado Pago ecosystem gives them a structural advantage in banking that $AMZN cannot replicate. They are deliberately squeezing their own margins today to fund an aggressive expansion of their logistics and credit books, effectively suffocating rivals and securing total ecosystem dominance.

Management is not trying to trick anybody. They are one of the most honest teams and have continuously said they are trading short-term margins for long-term value. This is the same team that recognized the limitations of their original asset-light model over a decade ago when Amazon first entered Brazil, aggressively pivoted their logistics, and successfully beat Amazon in Brazil and Mexico.

Automatic PIX Bot (ATS) & FaaS Model 

Technical analysis of recent videos circulating in fraud groups, cross-referenced with intelligence data, reveals a sophisticated Android malware being sold as a service (FaaS) to drain bank accounts in seconds.

Critical Intelligence Insights:

1️⃣ ATS (Automatic Transfer System) Capability: This malware is not just a screen-mirroring tool; it operates as an “Automatic PIX Thief.” Once active, it autonomously scans and executes transfers of the entire available balance, exploiting Accessibility Services and Screen Overlay permissions.

2️⃣ Advanced Evasion: The artifact is promoted as “100% undetectable,” claiming to bypass defenses like Google Play Protect and Kaspersky. It adapts to various social engineering flows (Fake Support/0800) to trick victims into granting critical permissions.

3️⃣ C2 Infrastructure: The “Art the Clown” panel (seen in the video) allows operators to swap destination PIX keys in real-time and monitor successful transfers. Major Brazilian fintechs and banks like PagSeguro, MercadoPago, Iti, C6, PicPay, and Neon are direct targets.

4️⃣ Cybercrime Economy: The business model is structured around rentals ranging from R￼￼￼ 1,000 (weekly), with additional social engineering modules sold separately for R$ 200.

⚠️ Note: This analysis is based on the visual context of the videos and collected marketing materials, without inferring actual effectiveness against all banking security layers.

Recommendation: Financial institutions should strengthen detection of Accessibility Service abuse and monitor rapid transaction patterns via ATS.
